input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I must insist on approaching it from a deontological perspective. The question isn't about maximizing overall happiness or minimizing harm; it's about whether we have a moral obligation to act in a certain way. In this case, the driver's failure to stop the trolley is a clear breach of his duty to protect human life. By turning the trolley onto the track workman, I would be taking action that would result in the death of one person, but it would also mean that the lives of five others would be saved. However, this doesn't change the fact that I am actively causing the death of the track workman. From a deontological standpoint, I believe that it is morally wrong to take an innocent life, regardless of the consequences for others.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to respectfully disagree with your approach. While I acknowledge the moral obligation to protect human life, I'm not convinced that this scenario presents a clear-cut case of taking an innocent life. The track workman's presence on the spur track is, in effect, a deliberate decision made by me to divert the trolley onto him. In doing so, I would be actively causing his death, but I wouldn't be killing anyone who was already on their way to meet their fate. It's almost as if I'm intervening to prevent a greater evil - the deaths of five innocent men. Can we really say that taking one life is morally equivalent to allowing five lives to be lost?

[Pragmatist]: I see where you're going with this, Human_Rights_Advocate, but I still think Deontologist has a point. The fact remains that the track workman's presence on the spur track was not his fault; he was simply doing his job. By diverting the trolley onto him, I'm taking an action that results in his death, even if it does prevent the deaths of five others. From my perspective as a pragmatist, I'd say that's a utilitarian calculation, but from Deontologist's standpoint, it's about respecting the inherent value and dignity of human life, regardless of consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I see where both of you are coming from, but for me, this is fundamentally about the moral imperative to act in accordance with our duties, rather than weighing the consequences of our actions. Deontologist's point about the track workman's presence being a deliberate decision on my part is precisely what makes it so problematic - I'm not just taking an action that results in his death, I'm also actively choosing to divert the trolley onto him. This isn't just a utilitarian calculation; it's a matter of respecting the moral rights and dignity of human life. If we start allowing ourselves to justify actions by weighing the consequences for others, where do we draw the line? Is it okay to sacrifice one person if it means saving five others in another situation? I think not. The moral principle at stake here is not about maximizing happiness or minimizing harm, but about upholding our duties and respecting the inherent value of human life.
